data.title: Cleveland-Cliffs reaches $12M DoJ settlement to address contamination at Middletown Works
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Justice said Tuesday it reached a proposed settlement with Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) that would require the company to address releases of hazardous waste at its Middletown Works steel plant in Ohio.

Under the proposed consent decree, Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) would be responsible for addressing closed landfills that historically received wastes from the mill, including industrial wastewater treatment and steel production process sludges; the corrective measures at the areas are expected to cost $12M, the DoJ said.

The company also would implement corrective measures at other site areas, such as the mill's production and slag processing areas.

The proposed settlement would close a civil lawsuit against the company's predecessor AK Steel by the DoJ, which was later joined by the state of Ohio and environmental groups.

data.title: Mondelez reaffirms 2026 EPS outlook as $350m+ inventory headwind hits Q1 and middle east costs rise
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Mondelez International (MDLZ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Dirk Van de Put (Chairman & CEO) said developed markets started the year “in line, maybe even slightly better than our expectations,” pointing to Europe where “the retailer negotiations are generally complete,” and adding, “We had a very robust Easter season, which share improvements in several of our markets.”

  • Quote (Chairman & CEO Dirk Van de Put): “The U.S., the consumer confidence there remains quite low. We expect it to further deteriorate as the Middle East conflict continues.” He added that in North America, “We gained some share in crackers led by strong performance of Ritz,” while “Oreo was a little bit less,” and management planned “to improve Oreo in the year to go.”

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & COO Luca Zaramella): “We are ahead of expectation in Q1… But on the remainder of the year… we need also to address some headwinds that we didn't have in our original forecast, particularly as they stem out of the Middle East crisis.”

Outlook

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & COO Luca Zaramella): “At this point in time, to be able to swallow it, we had to confirm guidance on the bottom line.” He also said that if “EPS upside materializes,” Mondelez would “most likely… invest it back in the business,” referencing “what we committed to, which is a strong 2027 EPS growth.”

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & COO Zaramella): “The team is managing that situation quite well, finding alternative routes to produce our brands and to deliver our brands, but that is coming at an extra cost.”

Financial Results

  • Quote (Chairman & CEO Dirk Van de Put): “It [emerging markets] remains very strong. It's about 40% of our business, as you know, we grew 6.3% in Q1.” He added, “Volume mix in emerging markets was up 0.5%.”

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & COO Luca Zaramella): “The headwind for the quarter is around about $350 million, a little bit more than that.” He said the quarter’s gross margin dynamics benefited from “volume mix… slightly positive,” growth in “quite profitable” markets including “China… grew 5%,” and “year-on-year benefits to the P&L” from productivity.

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & COO Zaramella): “We have also unlocked additional investments in a couple of places… So that's where we decided to invest more in A&C and other things.”

Q&A

  • Andrew Lazar, Barclays: asked about developed vs. emerging market drivers and share/volume inflections; Chair & CEO Van de Put: described Europe’s “very robust Easter season,” North America’s “slightly positive net revenue growth,” and emerging markets growth of “6.3% in Q1,” while flagging China as the only softer consumer among key EMs.

  • Peter Galbo, BofA Securities: asked why only reaffirm EPS despite a strong start and about “strong earnings growth for 2027”; Executive VP, CFO & COO Zaramella: said Middle East-related “extra cost” and oil impacts drove the decision to “confirm guidance on the bottom line,” and added any upside would likely be reinvested.

  • Peter Galbo, BofA Securities: asked whether European retailer negotiations were largely complete; Executive VP, CFO & COO Zaramella: said, “we are almost entirely done,” with only “a couple of small customers… nothing really material.”

  • Megan Christine Alexander, Morgan Stanley: asked about Europe’s competitive environment and cocoa volatility; Chair & CEO Van de Put: said, “We don't see any movements in price happening at the moment,” adding the market is waiting for cocoa’s second-half outlook.

  • Megan Christine Alexander, Morgan Stanley: asked for management’s current cocoa assessment; Executive VP, CFO & COO Zaramella: said “nothing has really fundamentally changed,” cited industry coverage “around about 10 months,” and added, “We believe 2,500… is a much better representation of what supply and demand would say.”

  • David Palmer, Evercore ISI: asked about Europe elasticity and what to watch in the consumer; Chair & CEO Van de Put: said, “we don't see anything in the consumer that would be something that preoccupies us,” while pointing to energy and inflation sensitivity if the conflict persists.

  • David Palmer, Evercore ISI: asked about gross margin and inventory phasing; Executive VP, CFO & COO Zaramella: confirmed the quarter headwind was “around about $350 million,” and tied the remainder-year setup to Middle East cost headwinds and incremental investment.

  • Michael Lavery, Piper Sandler: asked about innovation strategy and key focus areas; Chair & CEO Van de Put: emphasized “hit the right price points,” “in-store activations,” and “innovations that stand out,” highlighting protein/fiber, cakes & pastries, premium chocolate, and the “partnership… with Biscoff.”

  • Robert Moskow, TD Cowen: asked to reconcile weaker U.S. confidence with expected North America improvement; Chair & CEO Van de Put: described a “K-shaped economy” and channel shifts, while Executive VP, CFO & COO Zaramella said Mondelez expects “a volume and revenue inflection” later in the year driven by savory share gains and stronger execution in “Club” and “value.”

  • John Baumgartner, Mizuho: asked about the North America biscuits supply chain modernization; Executive VP, CFO & COO Zaramella: said “around about 60% of the network… is really state-of-the-art,” outlined simplifying underperforming plants, bringing co-manufactured platforms in-house, investing in packaging for channel-specific packs, and automating DSD fulfillment centers to “reduce our stock and reduce cost.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on whether Mondelez would raise guidance after a strong start, how cocoa could evolve, and what margin phasing implies.

  • Slightly positive management tone in fundamentals but cautious on geopolitics and costs, with Zaramella saying the company is “confident” while remaining “cautiously optimistic,” and emphasizing reinvestment if upside appears.

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management discussion appeared to shift from cocoa-price shock and competitive reaction risk to Middle East disruption costs and execution-led improvement, while analysts moved from “precipitous fall in cocoa” questions to guidance discipline and margin phasing.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q1 2026, management framed incremental headwinds around “extra cost” from “the Middle East crisis,” while Q4 2025 emphasized uncertainty from a sudden cocoa decline and potential “unexpected competitive reactions.”

  • Q1 2026 leaned more heavily into “reinvestments” and momentum (Biscoff, Easter, savory share), while Q4 2025 focused on rebuilding European chocolate price points and building “flexibility” into guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & COO Luca Zaramella): “finding alternative routes… is coming at an extra cost,” with oil dynamics also pressuring profitability in certain “regulated market[s]” where hedging protection is limited.

  • Quote (Chairman & CEO Dirk Van de Put): Europe’s consumer is “stable, but it's fragile,” and the key watch area is whether conflict-driven inflation (energy, packaging, oil) begins to change buying patterns.

Final Takeaway

Mondelez management described Q1 momentum across Europe and emerging markets, including 6.3% growth in emerging markets and improving share trends in developed markets, but reaffirmed its 2026 bottom-line outlook as it absorbed $350 million-plus inventory phasing headwinds and higher operating costs tied to Middle East disruption. Leadership repeatedly emphasized that any EPS upside would likely be reinvested to sustain brand momentum and support what it called “a strong 2027 EPS growth.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil jumps as U.A.E.'s OPEC exit outweighed by Strait of Hormuz shutdown
data.description:
Tensions between the United Arab Emirates and OPEC have been building well before Tuesday's sudden departure of the group's third-biggest producer, having chafed for years at OPEC's per-country production quotas that are intended to limit global supply and lift oil prices.

Saudi Arabia and the U.A.E. are the cartel's two swing producers that have excess capacity to ramp up output, with the U.A.E. capable of producing 1.4M bbl/day more than OPEC’s quota.

While OPEC's control over prices has waned, its quotas have hurt the U.A.E., which has a lower breakeven price than other OPEC members, and the country has clashed with the Saudis at times because it has been forced to bear a disproportionate share of the cartel's production cuts.

The U.A.E. has considered leaving the cartel for some time, and the Iran war may have provided a convenient cover; in any case, Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz has rendered OPEC quotas meaningless for now as the Persian Gulf states have been forced to slash production.

The U.A.E. can probably produce 4.5M-4.8M bbl/day sustainably once the strait reopens—well above its 3.4M bbl/day pre-war OPEC quota—but given the loss of 1B barrels of oil supply since the Middle East war started, "this won't be a huge headache for a while," Neil Crosby of Sparta Commodities said in a note.

"We believe this decision is a negative development for the cartel and could lead to increased oil supply entering the market after the war. It also reinforces a long-term bearish outlook on oil prices," Spartan Capital’s Peter Cardillo wrote.

On Tuesday, the bearish news of the U.A.E.'s exit from OPEC was outweighed by the continued stoppage of transit through the Strait of Hormuz.

"In normal times, this would have been very ​bearish news for the oil market and sparked a sizeable selloff," Again Capital's John Kilduff said.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for June delivery jumped 3.7% to $99.93/bbl, its highest close in three weeks and after trading above $100/bbl during the day, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery added 2.8% to $111.26/bbl, its highest settlement value since March 31.

Front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for May delivery edged 0.3% higher at $2.559/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Kilroy raises 2026 FFO guidance to $3.49-$3.63 as it plans 1900 Broadway with low-to-mid-9% stabilized yields
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kilroy Realty Corporation (KRC) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Over the last several quarters, fundamentals across our West Coast markets have meaningfully improved" as "return to office momentum has intensified" and "the artificial intelligence ecosystem has created considerable new business formation and growth" (CEO & Director Angela Aman).

  • "Our team's disciplined execution drove our strongest first quarter leasing results since 2017" with "total productivity of approximately 568,000 square feet" and "leases signed but not yet commenced" representing "nearly $78 million of contractually obligated annualized base rent" (CEO Aman).

  • "We sold 2 office properties... for aggregate gross proceeds of $146 million" and "subsequent to quarter end, we closed on the sale of our 2 Hollywood residential assets... for aggregate gross proceeds of $202 million"; "with proceeds... we... repurchas[ed] approximately $73 million of stock at an average price of $30.80 per share" and "fully redeemed the $50 million tranche of private placement notes" (CEO Aman).

  • "We... announced the formation of a joint venture to develop... 1900 Broadway" and "executed a 20-year lease... for 145,000 square feet"; "we intend to break ground next year" and the anchor tenant "is expected to take occupancy in early 2030" (EVP & Chief Investment Officer Eliott Trencher).

  • "FFO for the first quarter was $0.91 per diluted share"; "portfolio occupancy ended the quarter at 77.6%" and "excluding KOP 2... would have been 81.5%"; "we increased our 2026 FFO guidance... with a new FFO range of $3.49 to $3.63" (Treasurer, Executive VP & CFO Jeffrey Kuehling).

Outlook

  • "We increased our 2026 FFO guidance by $0.21 at the midpoint" to "$3.49 to $3.63" and "increase[d] our full year average occupancy guidance by 25 basis points at the midpoint" (CFO Kuehling; CEO Aman).

  • "Cash same-property NOI growth is now expected to range from 25 to 125 basis points" and management cited a "$5.9 million settlement related to the 23andMe bankruptcy" plus "strengthening fundamentals in our core operations" (CFO Kuehling).

  • "We have revised our expense capitalization assumptions for Flower Mart to reflect continued capitalization through the fourth quarter" and management said it now expects the process "to be completed late in the fourth quarter" (CEO Aman).

  • Compared with the prior call, management previously guided "2026 FFO... $3.25 to $3.45" and assumed Flower Mart capitalization would cease "at the end of June 2026" (CFO Kuehling, Q4 2025 call).

Financial results

  • "Cash same-property NOI increased 1.8% in the first quarter" and management said the change was "driven by lower bad debt expense" and other income items, "partially offset" by "free rent periods" (CFO Kuehling).

  • "GAAP spreads" were "negative 10.6%" and "cash spreads" were "negative 16.8%"; management attributed this to "2 leases in San Francisco... space that was vacant for longer than 12 months" while noting "leasing on space... vacant for less than 12 months performed well" with "positive GAAP spreads of 19.2%" and "cash spreads of 5.2%" (CFO Kuehling).

  • On 1900 Broadway, management disclosed "total anticipated cost... between $330 million and $350 million" and "stabilized yields... in the low to mid-9% range" (EVP Trencher).

Q&A

  • Manus (Evercore ISI): asked about L.A./San Diego recovery; Executive VP & Chief Leasing Officer A. Paratte said "we're seeing an increase in activity" and in L.A. "we signed 24 deals" and at Nautilus "had 400,000 square feet of tours since January 1."

  • Anthony Paolone (JPMorgan): asked about 1900 Broadway economics; EVP Trencher said "stabilized yields in the low to mid-9% range" and, on residential dispositions, said "the resi sales were around in the 4% range."

  • John Kim (BMO): questioned the signed-not-commenced mix and implied yield math; CEO Aman responded "it really is just a mix issue" and reiterated "stabilized yield... in the low to mid-9% range."

  • Nicholas Yulico (Scotiabank): pressed on West 8th and 360 Third/DIRECTV; A. Paratte said SoFi and GM at West 8th "are new to market" and CFO Kuehling clarified the 360 Third expiration is "a little over 100,000 square feet in Q2" and "that's a known vacate."

  • Blaine Heck (Wells Fargo): asked about KOP 2 pipeline and buybacks; CEO Aman said "the yield expectations... in the mid-5% range... are still fully intact" and, on repurchases, emphasized "prioritizing balance sheet strength and flexibility" (CEO Aman).

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical on development math and uncertainty, including, "I'm wondering if my math right?" and probing whether Flower Mart capitalization could continue beyond year-end (John Kim, BMO; William Catherwood, BTIG).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, including "we're really excited" on leasing and 1900 Broadway, while adding caution on forecasting: "I would love to be in the prediction business" (CEO Aman; Executive VP Paratte).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s guidance language shifted from initial 2026 framing (including June Flower Mart assumption) to a more explicitly revised outlook and capitalization timing update (CFO Kuehling, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Management moved from highlighting "fourth quarter leasing... approximately 827,000 square feet" and initial 2026 guidance to emphasizing "our strongest first quarter leasing results since 2017" and an updated 2026 outlook that lifted the FFO range (CEO Aman; CFO Kuehling, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).

  • Capital allocation emphasis broadened from a 2026 plan to sell operating assets to reporting year-to-date dispositions "approximately $350 million" and adding a new development JV with a long-dated lease and start date (CEO Aman; EVP Trencher).

Risks and concerns

  • "Q2 is by far our biggest move-out quarter during the course of 2026" and management said "the bulk of the 2026 remaining expirations will be move-outs" (CEO Aman).

  • On Flower Mart, management said the alternative city process "will take additional time" and while it expects a "pretty hard stop" to capitalization in late Q4, it acknowledged "a low probability, but not a 0% probability" of demand-driven changes later (CEO Aman).

Final takeaway

Management described improving West Coast office fundamentals tied to return-to-office and AI-driven demand, highlighted a step-up in leasing and visibility from signed-but-not-commenced rent, and updated 2026 guidance upward. Leadership also emphasized accelerated capital recycling (including office and residential dispositions), opportunistic share repurchases alongside debt repayment, and a new Redwood City development JV positioned around a long-term anchor lease, while reiterating that 2026 occupancy is expected to trough in Q2 due to known move-outs and that Flower Mart timing has been extended to late Q4 as the entitlement redesign process continues.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ultra clean projects Q2 revenue of $565M-$605M while expecting gross margin to improve through the year
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ultra Clean Holdings (UCTT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO James Xiao said the company “started the year out strong and delivered revenue and earnings above the midpoint of our guided range for the first quarter,” adding that “we're seeing momentum build across the semiconductor landscape, supported by growing industry-wide investments in AI-driven computing.”

  • Xiao tied demand to AI infrastructure, saying “hyperscalers and cloud providers expect to deploy significant data center capacity by spending around $600 billion in 2026,” and framed UCT’s positioning around “ramp readiness,” “MPX,” and “digital transformation” under “UCT 3.0.”

  • Xiao highlighted capacity and operating leverage, stating, “Our global footprint supports around $3 billion in revenue today and can scale up to $4 billion with modest incremental capital investment,” and added, “we do not expect infrastructure capacity to be our constraint.”

  • CFO Sheri Brumm said UCT is seeing “increased momentum from the early stages of a multiyear AI-driven expansion,” while staying focused on “operational efficiency, cost discipline and margin improvement” (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Sheri Brumm).

  • Brumm announced a leadership transition, saying, “After a lot of thought, I've decided to retire from UCT,” and added, “I'll remain fully engaged until we find my successor, looking both internally and externally” (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Brumm).

Outlook

  • Management guided Q2 total revenue to “between $565 million and $605 million” and EPS to “$0.44 to $0.60” (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Sheri Brumm).

  • Brumm said margin should improve as volumes rise: “Gross margin should start to continue to improve as we move through the year,” with Q2 “slightly up” and improvement continuing “as we get closer to the Q4 time frame,” while noting mix and shipping region can change the trajectory (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Brumm).

  • Compared with the prior quarter, Q1 guidance previously called for revenue of $505 million-$545 million and EPS of $0.18-$0.34, while the current call emphasized building momentum into the second half and “broad-based improvements across the semiconductor landscape heading into the second half of this year and beyond” (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Brumm).

  • Analysts’ estimates were provided in the prompt, but the data format did not match the required fiscal-quarter validation fields, so no estimate comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • UCT reported Q1 total revenue of $533.7 million, with product revenue of $465.7 million and services revenue of $68.0 million (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Sheri Brumm).

  • Brumm reported Q1 gross margin of 16.5% and operating margin of 5.1%, and said gross margin “improved primarily due to better product mix and higher volumes, driving factory efficiencies,” while cautioning that margins “continue to be influenced by fluctuations in volume, mix and manufacturing region as well as material and transportation costs” (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Brumm).

  • Brumm reported non-GAAP EPS of $0.31 on net income of $14.5 million and said operating cash flow was negative $33.3 million, “driven primarily by higher working capital as we build inventory to meet near-term demand and support future growth” (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Brumm).

  • Brumm detailed financing actions: “In February, we priced a $600 million offering of zero-coupon convertible senior notes,” used proceeds to repay Term Loan B, and said these steps are expected to reduce the weighted average borrowing rate “from around 6.2% to approximately 1.4%,” including refinancing and upsizing the revolver to $250 million with maturity extended to 2031 (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Brumm).

Q&A

  • Yu Shi, Needham: Asked for WFE outlook and the memory-to-foundry context; CEO James Xiao said customers are quoting “$140 billion to $145 billion in 2026,” referenced “18% to 20% year-over-year growth,” and added customers are discussing “15% and above for the 2027,” while describing memory investment as unlocking constrained capacity and pointing to “new leading-edge new factory launches in basically all 3 leading customers, TSMC, Intel and Samsung” (CEO Xiao).

  • Yu Shi, Needham: Asked what remains slow (including lithography and China); Xiao said the “fast-growing segment” is “leading-edge foundry logic and HBM… and advanced packaging,” while “the flattish area… is probably the non-dep and etch segment,” and said China’s portion of worldwide WFE moved “back to normal in the low 20s” after being “35 to 40” (CEO Xiao).

  • Yu Shi, Needham: Asked how UCT handles potentially unreasonable customer requests; Xiao said, “we see the customer actually giving us a long-term forecast,” and reiterated capacity from “$3 billion run rate” with “brick-and-mortar capacity… up to $4 billion” with “6 to 9 months to build that capacity” (CEO Xiao).

  • Robert Mertens, TD Cowen: Asked China revenue mix; Xiao said domestic China business is “less than 5% of our overall revenue,” and said UCT sees opportunity as domestic Chinese WFE customers increase share within China (CEO Xiao).

  • Christian Schwab, Craig-Hallum: Asked second-half weighting and growth cadence; Xiao pointed to “close to double-digit growth quarter-over-quarter from Q1 to Q2” and said UCT expects “a similar range of growth going forward and for the second half” (CEO Xiao).

  • Christian Schwab, Craig-Hallum: Asked services mix at $4 billion; Xiao said service revenue is a function of wafer starts, expects “double-digit growth for the year on the service side,” and sees services at “10% to 12% as our overall revenue percentage” (CEO Xiao).

  • Edward Yang, Oppenheimer: Asked gross margin progression; Brumm said gross margin should continue improving through the year, with variability driven by “mix and where it's shipping from” (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Brumm). Yang also asked for product margin ranges; Brumm said margins can range “between 10% to 50% to 60%” depending on whether it is “a component part or it's a module or a gas panel” (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Brumm). Yang asked about UCT 3.0/MPX traction; Xiao said regional centers of excellence are “well received by customer,” with “some early momentum,” and “a pretty strong pipeline of NPI engagement” (CEO Xiao).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive but probing, pressing on WFE visibility, China exposure, second-half weighting, and margin mechanics, including direct challenges such as “how should we think about gross margin progression?” and requests for specificity on margin differentials and market-share timing (Edward Yang, Oppenheimer; Yu Shi, Needham).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and expansion-focused, emphasizing confidence signals such as “we're aligned with our customers and industry sentiment that we're in the early stage of a multiyear cycle,” while adding caveats about “variability across customer spending, potential supply chain constraints and geopolitics” (CEO James Xiao).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on CEO transition (“This is my first sole earnings call as CEO”) and framing an “AI technology inflection,” the current call added a major executive-change catalyst with the CFO retirement announcement and more detailed capital-structure actions (CEO Xiao; Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Sheri Brumm).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter reinforced UCT 3.0 execution but shifted from broad “structural expansion” messaging to more explicit capacity and scaling language, including that the company can scale from $3 billion to $4 billion revenue capacity “with modest incremental capital investment,” and that capacity is not expected to be the limiting factor (CEO James Xiao).

  • Guidance stepped up sequentially from the prior call’s Q1 revenue of $505 million-$545 million and EPS of $0.18-$0.34 to the current call’s Q2 revenue of $565 million-$605 million and EPS of $0.44-$0.60 (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Sheri Brumm).

  • Analysts kept recurring pressure points—WFE outlook shape, China mix, and margin trajectory—but the current Q&A leaned more into operational scaling (forecast reasonableness, lead times, and capacity build windows) and margin composition detail (broad margin “bell curve” by product type) (CEO Xiao; Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Brumm).

Risks and Concerns

  • Xiao said “the near-term environment remains dynamic with variability across customer spending, potential supply chain constraints and geopolitics,” and also flagged “emerging supply side constraints, including clean room capacity and the time required to bring new fabs online” (CEO James Xiao).

  • Brumm noted margin volatility drivers including “volume, mix and manufacturing region as well as material and transportation costs,” and tied the quarter’s negative operating cash flow to working-capital build for inventory ahead of demand (Chief Financial Officer & Senior VP of Finance Sheri Brumm).

Final Takeaway

UCT management positioned Q1 as an early-stage ramp into a “multiyear AI-driven expansion,” guided Q2 to $565 million-$605 million in revenue and $0.44-$0.60 in EPS, and described a path to improving gross margin as volume rises through the year. Alongside operating updates on UCT 3.0 (ramp readiness, MPX, and digital transformation), the company highlighted a balance-sheet reset—$600 million zero-coupon converts, Term Loan B repayment, and a $250 million revolver refinancing—while also disclosing a CFO retirement and succession search.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: JetBlue suspends full-year guidance amid fuel spike, targets 100% fuel recapture by early 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: JetBlue Airways (JBLU) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Given the sharp increase in the price of fuel and the expectation for elevated prices throughout this year, we are suspending our prior full year guidance,” said Joanna Geraghty (CEO Joanna Geraghty), adding, “We have taken immediate action to offset fuel costs, with our ultimate focus on minimizing the financial impact and preserving our liquidity position.”
  • “The 3 primary levers available to us are: adjusting fares to better align with input costs, moderating unproductive capacity and pursuing additional cost savings opportunities,” Geraghty said, while also stating, “We expect 30% to 40% fuel recapture in the second quarter and plan to achieve 100% recapture by early 2027.”
  • “We delivered strong RASM performance of positive 6.5% in the first quarter,” said Martin St. George (President Martin St. George), and added that “Premium continued to outperform core, with year-over-year premium RASM better than core by 9 points in the first quarter.”
  • “We ended the quarter with $2.4 billion of liquidity or 26% of trailing 12-month revenue, above our liquidity target of 17% to 20%,” said Ursula Hurley (Chief Financial Officer Ursula Hurley), adding, “Earlier this month, we raised $500 million secured by aircraft collateral with an accordion feature that allows us to upsize to $750 million.”

Outlook

  • “For the second quarter, we expect RASM to grow 7% to 11% year-over-year on 1.5% to 4.5% more capacity,” St. George said, while noting “The Easter outbound shift represents a second quarter headwind of about 1.5 points of RASM.”
  • “We’ve acted quickly, reducing capacity by nearly 1 point versus close-in expectations in the second quarter, with plans to reduce the second half by at least 2 to 3 points,” Geraghty said.
  • “We expect second quarter fuel price to be in the range of $4.13 to $4.28,” Hurley said, adding, “every $0.10 increase or decrease in fuel price is the equivalent to about $85 million of expense for the full year.”
  • Compared with the prior call’s full-year framing (“our forecast of breakeven operating margin or better,” per CEO Geraghty in Q4 2025), management replaced full-year targets with near-term actions and said, “As we gain greater visibility into fuel and its impact on the macro environment, we will plan to provide an updated view on full year expectations.”

Financial Results

  • “CASM ex-fuel growth finished up 6.6%,” Hurley said, and added, “For the second quarter, we expect CASM ex-fuel to increase in the range of 3% to 5% year-over-year.”
  • “Average fuel price for the first quarter was $2.96,” Hurley said, adding it was “26% higher than the midpoint of our initial guidance.”
  • “In the first quarter, capital expenditures totaled $141 million,” Hurley said, adding, “we expect approximately $275 million of capital expenditures in the second quarter and approximately $800 million in 2026,” and “we now expect 12 total aircraft deliveries this year, down from our January guidance of 14 aircraft.”

Q&A

  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank: asked about domestic first class sales timing and early uptake; President St. George answered, “No, we have not begun selling it yet,” and CEO Geraghty added, “We’re currently going through the certification process.”
  • Conor Cunningham, Melius: pressed on fuel recapture timing vs fare increases; CEO Geraghty said, “1Q, we were 90% booked,” and added, “Going forward, once those fare increases started going in a very different story.” Cunningham also asked about the $250M accordion; CFO Hurley said, “we will most likely draw down on that in order to maintain our 17% to 20% liquidity target.”
  • Daniel McKenzie, Seaport: asked about incremental liquidity options; CFO Hurley said, “if we are at risk of falling below our liquidity level, we could decide to lever up those new deliveries,” and added, “we have options.” McKenzie also asked on demand strength/elasticity; President St. George said, “the revenue environment has been extremely robust even in the face of pretty high fare increases.”
  • Jamie Baker, JPMorgan: asked about Q3 revenue probability; President St. George said, “We’re not going to guide third quarter,” while adding, “we remain optimistic that we will continue as the year progresses, to start recovering more and more of the increased price of fuel.” Baker also asked about bailout requests; CEO Geraghty said, “Never say never. We’re open to anything and everything, assuming the terms would make sense for JetBlue.”
  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: asked whether a Spirit support outcome changes consolidation thinking; CEO Geraghty said, “No,” and added, “We’re now the #1 carrier in Fort Lauderdale.” Pfennigwerth also asked where cuts focus; President St. George said, “it generally is off-peak periods, Tuesday, Wednesday, stuff like that.”
  • Savanthi Syth, Raymond James: asked how far along Fort Lauderdale build is; President St. George said, “we’ll absolutely continue to build Fort Lauderdale to the extent we can,” and added, “a lot of that is going to be predicated on gate availability.”
  • Michael Goldie, BMO: asked about card performance by region and Paisly partners; President St. George said, “the cards are basically New York, New Jersey, New England,” and added, “We have talked to single-digit number of other entities.”
  • Thomas Fitzgerald, TD Cowen: asked about Blue Sky customer response and mixed-metal connections; President St. George said, “it’s exactly what we expected,” and added, “We don’t have a date for that yet because that’s actually a bit of a technology challenge.”
  • Brandon Oglenski, Barclays: asked how JetBlue addresses scale; CEO Geraghty said, “we’ve pivoted more focus on Blue Sky,” and added, “Paisly is the other piece of the puzzle.”
  • Atul Maheswari, UBS: asked why recapture appears lower vs legacy peers; President St. George said, “I’m not sure I agree with that,” while CEO Geraghty added, “we think it’s maybe premium and corporate mix.”
  • Catherine O’Brien, Goldman Sachs: asked about Premier Card contribution and cost cadence; President St. George said, “we don’t release a lot of detail,” while CFO Hurley said, “our unit cost will be over 2 points less in 2H versus 1H.”
  • Madison Pasterchick, Morgan Stanley: asked about Europe exposure amid fuel concerns; CFO Hurley said, “It’s only 6% of our ASMs,” adding, “the exposure is minimal for us.”
  • John Godyn, Citigroup: challenged capacity-cut philosophy vs fuel curve assumptions; President St. George said, “Our goal is to contribute as much to getting to positive EBIT as we can,” and added, “we look at the fuel curve and wonder how realistic it is.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to negative, with Barclays calling it “another frustrating year” and Citi pressing, “It doesn’t seem like that’s being contemplated in a serious way.”
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive on demand and initiatives but defensive on volatility and guidance, including “I want to be clear, suspending our full year guidance reflects external factors alone” (CEO Geraghty).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s more confident framing (“we entered 2026 focused, energized and committed to returning to breakeven profitability or better,” said CFO Hurley in Q4 2025), the current call emphasized uncertainty and flexibility, including “it’s anybody’s guess where fuel is going to be.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest shift was guidance: Q4 2025 provided a full-year framework (“breakeven operating margin or better,” per CEO Geraghty) while Q1 2026 said, “we are suspending our prior full year guidance.”
  • Network and commercial strategy remained consistent but became more cost-and-fuel centric, with Q1 2026 stressing “fare,” “capacity,” and “costs,” and adding explicit fuel-recapture milestones (“100% recapture by early 2027”).
  • Analyst focus shifted from JetForward ramp mechanics (Q4) to liquidity, capital-raising, and fuel-driven downside scenarios (Q1), while management reiterated “JetForward remains the right strategy.”

Risks and Concerns

  • “The conflict in the Middle East and its impact on fuel prices is the most significant headwind we faced as an industry since COVID,” CEO Geraghty said.
  • CFO Hurley highlighted sensitivity and mitigation actions: “every $0.10 increase or decrease in fuel price is the equivalent to about $85 million of expense for the full year,” alongside “reducing spend across both OpEx and CapEx and slowing hiring in some work groups.”
  • Management also cited operational and network constraints, including President St. George’s note, “we do have some constraints with our slot base at JFK.”

Final Takeaway

JetBlue management said it suspended full-year guidance after a sharp fuel spike tied to the Middle East conflict, while leaning on fare actions, capacity reductions, and incremental cost initiatives to protect liquidity and “preserv[e] our liquidity position.” The company guided to Q2 RASM growth of 7% to 11% and said it expects 30% to 40% fuel-cost recapture in Q2, aiming for 100% recapture by early 2027, while also signaling additional flexibility on capacity and financing (including potential use of the $250M accordion) if elevated fuel persists.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Stride narrows FY 2026 outlook to $2.490B-$2.520B revenue and $490M-$500M adjusted operating income
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stride, Inc. (LRN) Q3 FY 2026

Management view

  • "I'd like to start off with an update on the platform issues we experienced earlier this year." (Chair of the Board & CEO James Rhyu) "We continue to execute on the road map for stability and improvements... So I'm comfortable with our progress and believe we are on the right track as we prepare for the fall season."

  • "The demand for our products and services, as indicated by application volumes continues to be strong relative to historic levels." (Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu) "Although we experienced a marginally higher level of attrition since I gave an update on our last call, this was not unexpected... we are confident it won't harm our long-term prospects."

  • "Total enrollments grew 1.8% to 244,500 and total revenue for the quarter was $629.9 million, up 2.7% compared to last year." (Executive VP & CFO Donna Blackman) "Revenue in our career learning, middle and high school programs grew nearly 16% to $259.5 million... General Education revenue was $357.5 million, down 3.6% compared to last year."

  • "Gross margins at 36.8% for the quarter was down 380 basis points." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman) "Selling, general and administrative expenses totaled $102.5 million, down $16 million or 13.5% from last year... adjusted earnings per share for the quarter was $2.30."

Outlook

  • "We should have a better picture of the enrollment landscape during the fourth quarter call as well as more color on the funding environment as states finalize their budgets in the coming months." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman)

  • "For the full year, we are narrowing our revenue, AOI and CapEx guidance ranges." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman) "For the balance of the year, we expect revenue in the range of $2.490 billion to $2.520 billion, narrowed from $2.480 billion to $2.555 billion last quarter" and "adjusted operating income between $490 million and $500 million narrowed from $485 million and $505 million last quarter."

  • "Capital expenditures between $75 million and $80 million, narrowed from $70 million and $80 million last quarter and an effective tax rate between 24% and 25%, unchanged from last quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman)

  • "As you'll note, the range of revenue implies fourth quarter revenue below the fourth quarter of last year, driven by marginally higher attrition rates and tough comparisons associated with the timing of funding true-ups." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman) "We do not, however, believe this is indicative of any change in underlying demand trends."

Financial results

  • "Total revenue per enrollment across both Career Learning and General Education was $2,485, up 2.9% from $2,415 last year." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman) "Given this quarter's results, we expect total revenue per enrollment for the full year to be up roughly 2% from last year."

  • "We would expect a portion of these costs to moderate as we move into FY 2027." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman) "The year-over-year decline is primarily driven by continued investments in the business, including those related to our platform rollout as we support the transition."

  • "Adjusted operating income was $140.4 million, down 1%." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman) "Adjusted EBITDA was $171.3 million, up 1.8%."

  • "Free cash flow, defined as cash from operations less CapEx, was $202.4 million, up from $37.3 million last year." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman) "For the year, we expect free cash flow to be flattish to last year" and "we finished the quarter with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $856 million."

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: "comment on the contract renewal pipeline or new business" and whether there was "pushback" from prior issues; Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu: "our pipeline of new business activity is... as strong or stronger than it's been in the 5 years that I've been the CEO" and "we have seen no negative impact" on "doors that are opening for us."

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: whether Pennsylvania funding changes signal a broader trend; Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu: "I don't think we see any different trend than the activity we've seen in the past 10 or 15 years" and on Pennsylvania, "these types of situations present opportunities for stronger players like us."

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: whether enrollment windows "closed earlier" and impact; Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu: "the short answer is yes" and "we generally this year have taken the stance of trying to backfill as opposed to grow," adding the volume left unconverted was "clearly in the thousands."

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: whether strong applications translate into waitlists and fall pipeline; Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu: "In some cases, yes, they get added to a waitlist" and "we've actually started opening up enrollment for the fall" with "provisional state enrollments."

  • Matthew Filek, William Blair: marketing and SG&A posture as platform stabilizes; Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu: "we're on a trajectory for essentially business as usual" and "we're going to be in the market pretty aggressively," also noting "data points" where "customer use of AI might be actually improving conversion" and "our cost of acquisition actually goes down."

  • Matthew Filek, William Blair: adult learning path back to growth; Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu: "the boot camps are in just secular decline" and "the MedCerts business is and continues to be an attractive market segment for us," but "we have not executed well" and "we've had some leadership changes."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative to neutral: questions repeatedly pressed on platform fallout, funding risk, enrollment-window constraints, and marketing intensity, including "pushback" concerns and whether states are "changing how they're funding virtual schools" (Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research) and quantifying students "left on the table" (Alexander Paris, Barrington Research).

  • Management slightly positive in prepared remarks, more explanatory in Q&A: "I'm comfortable with our progress" (Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu) and "we remain optimistic about the coming school year" (Executive VP & CFO Blackman), while using careful framing on uncertainty: "it is still very early in the enrollment season" (Executive VP & CFO Blackman).

  • Versus last quarter, language was less absolute on platform resolution: Q2 included "the core issues are behind us" and "I'm confident we will not have a recurrence" (Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu), while Q3 emphasized execution and preparation: "we continue to execute on the road map" and "we are heads down" (Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance shifted from reaffirmation and added Q3 ranges in Q2 to tightened full-year ranges in Q3: Q2 said, "we are reaffirming our full year revenue guidance of $2.480 billion to $2.555 billion" and gave Q3 ranges; Q3 said, "we are narrowing our revenue, AOI and CapEx guidance ranges" to "$2.490 billion to $2.520 billion" revenue and "$490 million and $500 million" adjusted operating income (Executive VP & CFO Blackman).

  • Platform narrative moved from resolution claims to ongoing work and attrition acknowledgment: Q2 said "the core issues are behind us" (Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu); Q3 said "we experienced a marginally higher level of attrition... this was not unexpected" and emphasized fall readiness (Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu).

  • Analyst focus remained centered on platform aftereffects and demand conversion; Q3 sharpened on enrollment-window timing and state funding (Pennsylvania), while Q2 leaned more on whether stabilized withdrawals allowed renewed in-year growth.

Risks and concerns

  • "Marginally higher attrition" and fourth-quarter revenue pressure were explicitly linked to "marginally higher attrition rates" and "tough comparisons associated with the timing of funding true-ups." (Executive VP & CFO Blackman)

  • Platform execution risk remained central: "we continue to execute on the road map for stability and improvements" and the company was "heads down" ahead of fall (Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu).

  • Policy/funding uncertainty was raised in Q&A: "there's always legislation that's getting proposed, both for and against virtual programs" (Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu), with Pennsylvania framed as both disruption and "an opportunity" for "stronger players" (Chair of the Board & CEO Rhyu).

Final takeaway

Management framed Q3 as a quarter of steady demand signals but tighter near-term constraints: platform remediation remained a priority heading into the fall, application volumes were described as strong, and guidance was narrowed with an explicit callout that the implied Q4 revenue range reflected higher attrition and difficult comparisons tied to funding true-ups rather than a change in underlying demand. The call’s Q&A concentrated on whether last year’s platform issues were affecting partner renewals, new-business pipeline, enrollment-window dynamics, and state funding, while management repeatedly characterized new-business conversations as robust and positioned marketing plans as returning to “business as usual.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CECO targets $940M-$1B 2026 sales as it expects early June Thermon close
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CECO Environmental (CECO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are off to a strong start to 2026" and "we delivered another strong quarter with numerous financial records" including "tremendous orders" that "increased our backlog to new levels," according to CEO Todd Gleason.
  • Gleason said, "we are raising our full year 2026 outlook, not inclusive of Thermon," adding, "this is our second guidance raise this year," while noting "uncertainty related to the Iran war and modestly higher inflation."
  • On Thermon, Gleason stated, "We remain on track for a Q2 close, and our current expectation is sometime in early June," and "we remain highly confident in the $40 million cost synergies we previously outlined."
  • CFO Peter Johansson reported, "We finished the first quarter with a record backlog of $1.035 billion" and "first quarter orders were $449 million," while adding, "Revenue in the first quarter was $206 million" and "Adjusted EBITDA was $20.4 million in the quarter."

Outlook

  • Gleason said, "Our updated revenue outlook now expects sales to be between $940 million and $1 billion" and "we are also increasing our adjusted EBITDA outlook to $120 million to $140 million," while emphasizing the raise is "not inclusive of Thermon."
  • Gleason tied the raise to visibility, saying, "Our growth trajectory remains robust" with "nice visibility to our revenue and margin profile in our record backlog as well as larger-than-ever sales pipeline."
  • Johansson set expectations for profitability progression, stating, "We expect margins to improve in the second quarter and trend back towards our target gross profit margin level of 34% or greater as we progress throughout the year."

Financial Results

  • Johansson said backlog rose "$242 million sequentially" to "a record backlog of $1.035 billion," and described Q1 as "typically CECO's seasonally smallest revenue quarter" while adding, "we are confident to deliver sequential revenue increases throughout 2026."
  • On margin dynamics, Johansson stated, "Margins, however, did experience contraction in quarter 1," attributing it to "the sale of the higher-margin but nonstrategic global pump business" and "the revenue timing of lower-margin jobs booked in early 2025."
  • On cash and leverage, Johansson said, "we consumed approximately $16 million of cash" and noted "a customer payment of nearly $20 million that was delayed but already received here early in the second quarter"; he added the leverage ratio was "2.3x" and that CECO now has "up to $975 million in committed funds" under its amended credit agreement.

Q&A

  • Aaron Spychalla, Craig-Hallum: asked about pipeline drivers, power-gen pipeline, delivery timelines, and supply chain readiness; CEO Gleason responded, "the $7 billion, it's actually about $7.3 billion" and said April bookings were accelerating, while adding on supply chain, "you don't do so without a great supply chain" and "we aggressively prebuy or...lock in rates."
  • Spychalla, Craig-Hallum: asked about Middle East impacts and water timing; Gleason said, "it's obviously an uncertain time" and "we have...programs in our pipeline that have been paused a bit until probably the second half of this year," while adding, "in our guidance, we have already accounted for any of those impacts."
  • Gerard Sweeney, ROTH: asked how far out power visibility extends; CFO Johansson answered, "we're today receiving...questions for orders that will deliver in 2029 and 2030" and "we essentially are done for the 2027 and 2028 installs."
  • Sweeney, ROTH: asked about pricing and margins; Johansson said, "Price for us is a lever to increase margins" and noted inflation protections via "escalators" and commodity exposure including "the catalyst" and "specialty steels."
  • Robert Brown, Lake Street: asked whether industrial water strength is market-driven; Gleason said it was "probably more our participation and entrance into the market," while also citing "water scarcity" and reuse as key drivers.
  • Brown, Lake Street: asked about Thermon commercial synergies; Gleason said, "We really haven't put a number around it yet" but added, "can the combination add a couple of points of organic growth? I believe so, yes."
  • Robert Brooks, Northland: asked what’s strongest beyond power; Gleason highlighted "semiconductor" and "industrial water," while Johansson added, "The natural gas infrastructure whole value chain is also a very -- a market of strength globally."
  • James Ricchiuti, Needham: pressed on gross margin improvement; Johansson said, "There's 3 factors" including project timing, "higher margins" on newer projects, and "continue to improve our G&A cost footprint" toward "34% or greater target."
  • Christopher Grenga, TD Cowen: asked about 80/20 scope and tariff exposure; Johansson said "about 10% of CECO's revenue" is in Wave 1 and could reach "20%, 25%" by end of summer, and on tariffs, "We haven't identified any material impact" due to in-region sourcing and "USMCA exemptions."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive but probing on durability and execution capacity, including questions framed around long-cycle visibility and constraints such as turbines "sold out to 2029" (Gerard Sweeney, ROTH Capital Partners, LLC).
  • Management tone was positive and confident in momentum and near-term orders, including "We know it" on Q2 orders (CEO Todd Gleason) and "we are confident to deliver sequential revenue increases throughout 2026" (CFO Peter Johansson).
  • Versus last quarter’s prepared emphasis on announcing the Thermon deal, the current quarter leaned more heavily on operating momentum and order acceleration, including "April alone, we already booked over $400 million in new orders" (CEO Gleason).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from a first raise in Q4 to a second raise in Q1, with Gleason stating in Q1, "this is our second guidance raise this year," following Q4’s statement, "we are raising our full year 2026 guidance."
  • Orders and backlog discussion escalated from Q4’s "approaching $800 million" backlog and "largest ever project valued at approximately $135 million" to Q1’s "record backlog of $1.035 billion" and "our largest ever order...in the range of $300 million" (CEO Gleason).
  • Thermon timeline moved from Q4’s close expected "in mid-2026" to Q1’s "current expectation is sometime in early June" (CEO Gleason), while reiterating "$40 million" synergy confidence.

Risks and Concerns

  • Gleason cited macro and geopolitical uncertainty, referencing "uncertainty related to the Iran war and modestly higher inflation," and said some Middle East pursuits were "paused...until probably the second half of this year" while adding, "we feel comfortable with our guidance and our outlook."
  • Johansson flagged working-capital volatility and transaction costs, stating, "Working capital was a headwind" and CECO incurred "material costs and cash expenses related to the Thermon transaction," while expecting cash flow "to revert back to a positive state" in Q2.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized record order momentum feeding a $1.035B backlog, raised 2026 stand-alone guidance to $940M-$1B of sales and $120M-$140M of adjusted EBITDA, and pointed to improving margins later in the year as project mix and execution timing normalize. Leadership also reiterated confidence in closing the Thermon transaction in early June and in achieving the previously outlined $40M cost synergies, while acknowledging geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East and near-term working-capital pressure tied to growth and deal-related spending.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: COPT Defense raises 2026 FFO per share midpoint to $2.76 while committing $290M to new investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: COPT Defense Properties (CDP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We're off to a solid start in 2026, and all aspects of the business are on track to achieve our objectives for the year." (President, CEO & Trustee Stephen E. Budorick).

  • "For the first quarter, FFO per share was $0.69, which is $0.01 above the midpoint of guidance and represents a 6.2% year-over-year increase." (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick).

  • "We executed 1.2 million square feet of renewal leasing and achieved a 91% retention rate." (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick).

  • "We committed $55 million to 150,000 square foot development project at Redstone Gateway... This investment creates anti-terrorism force protective inventory or ATFP, for the United States government in advance of expected requirements." (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick).

  • "We committed roughly $43 million to the acquisition of 17 acres of land in a ground lease in the Westfield submarket in Chantilly, Virginia." (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick).

  • "We finished the quarter with strong occupancy at 94.4% in the total portfolio and 95.6% in the Defense/IT portfolio." (Executive VP & COO Britt Snider).

  • "We executed 1.2 million square feet in the quarter with tenant retention of 91%, cash rent spreads up 3.8% and GAAP rent spreads up 12%." (Executive VP & COO Snider).

  • "We increased the midpoint of FFO per share guidance by $0.01 to $2.76" (Executive VP & CFO Anthony Mifsud).

Outlook

  • "We increased the midpoint of FFO per share guidance by $0.01 to $2.76" and management said it was "driven by the contribution from both the outperformance during the quarter and the Mission Ridge land acquisition, partially offset by the accounting treatment for the dilution from our exchangeable notes." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

  • "We increased the midpoint of Same Property cash NOI growth by 50 basis points to 3%" and management tied that to "stronger renewal leasing and unanticipated real estate tax refunds." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

  • "We increased the midpoint of tenant retention guidance by 250 basis points to 82.5%." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

  • "We increased the midpoint of capital committed to new investment guidance by $40 million to $290 million due to the Mission Ridge land acquisition." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

  • "We're establishing second quarter guidance for FFO per share in a range of $0.68 to $0.70." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

Financial Results

  • "We reported first quarter FFO per share of $0.69" and management said results benefited from "earlier than budgeted commencement of several leases, strong renewal leasing, the timing of certain R&M projects and unbudgeted real estate tax refunds" and were "partially offset by higher-than-forecasted net winter weather-related expenses." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

  • "Same Property Cash NOI increased 5.4% year-over-year" and management said this was "driven by the burn-off of free rent on development and acquisition leases" and "a 70 basis point increase in Same Property average occupancy." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

  • "On March 16, we repaid our $400 million bond, which carried an interest rate of 2.25%" and management said the prefunding was done with "$400 million of 5-year unsecured notes at 4.5%," adding that "The increased interest on this $400 million of debt results in $0.09 of higher financing costs in 2026." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

  • "Moody's upgraded our investment-grade credit rating in March to Baa2" and management added, "We have no significant near-term refinancing risk as our next bond maturity is not until the fall of 2028." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

Q&A

  • Seth Bergey, Citi: Asked whether the long-term "4.5%" FFO per share growth rate could accelerate with defense spending; President, CEO & Trustee Budorick: "This year, our growth is a little muted because of the $0.09 of additional interest expense... So we expect to get somewhere around 1.5% of growth... hypothetically, there could be upside to that from the increase in defense spending" but added, "that bill has not even been passed and appropriated yet."

  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: Asked if development starts will change given tailwinds; President, CEO & Trustee Budorick: "We're not yet ready to start accumulating more inventory than we traditionally have" but said the company is "putting ourselves in a posture to move extremely quickly."

  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo: Asked if the defense budget size changes the leasing lag; President, CEO & Trustee Budorick: "I think the 12 to 18 months is still going to hold in."

  • William Catherwood, BTIG: Asked about net effective rent growth and concessions; Executive VP & CFO Mifsud: said management has "an enhanced focus" on net effective rents and concessions, and an executive added, "Yes, probably mid-single digits."

  • Richard Anderson, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about Des Moines data shells; President, CEO & Trustee Budorick: "We're at an impasse on power... we continue to think that 3 to 4 years out."

  • Dylan Burzinski, Green Street: Asked about acquisition pipeline; President, CEO & Trustee Budorick: "So we're currently not looking at anything, and it continues to be one-off."

  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan: Asked how much of the development leasing pipeline is tied to existing space vs. incremental starts; President, CEO & Trustee Budorick: "I'd say 25% to 30% are for things that we're actually building currently."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive and opportunity-focused around defense budget upside and growth durability, including "do you kind of see a path to that accelerating" (Seth Bergey, Citi) and "given that the increase is so substantial" (Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo).

  • Management tone was slightly positive but repeatedly conditional on appropriations and visibility, including "it's aspirational" and "it hasn't been passed yet nor appropriated." (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick).

  • Versus last quarter, management again emphasized the defense-funding lag, with "12 to 18 months" repeated in both calls, while Q1 included more explicit caution that FY 2027 spending "has not even been passed and appropriated yet." (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from Q4 establishing full-year 2026 FFO per share guidance at "a range of $2.71 to $2.79" (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud, Q4) to Q1 lifting the annual midpoint "to $2.76" and adding explicit "second quarter guidance" of "$0.68 to $0.70." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud, Q1).

  • Investment positioning broadened in Q1 with the "ground lease" structure at Mission Ridge and an inside-the-fence Redstone development framed as "ATFP" inventory (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick), while Q4 emphasized multiple "fully pre-leased" build-to-suit commitments (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick).

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4 emphasis on Golden Dome-related development visibility and funding acceleration to Q1 emphasis on long-term growth rate durability, Westfield submarket expansion, and the timing impact of budget passage.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The increased interest on this $400 million of debt results in $0.09 of higher financing costs in 2026." (Executive VP & CFO Mifsud).

  • On FY 2027 defense budget translation to demand, management said, "it's too early" and reiterated, "it hasn't been passed yet nor appropriated" and that contractor flow-through supports a "12 to 18" month pattern. (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick).

  • On Des Moines data center shells, management said, "We're at an impasse on power" and that moving forward under current terms was "too burdensome." (President, CEO & Trustee Budorick).

Final Takeaway

Management described a Q1 start that supported raising the 2026 FFO per share midpoint to $2.76, alongside higher same-property cash NOI growth and tenant retention assumptions, while committing additional capital to an inside-the-fence Redstone Arsenal development and a Northern Virginia ground lease. Executives repeatedly framed FY 2027 defense spending as a multi-step process, stressing that demand upside depends on budget passage and appropriations, and they highlighted near-term headwinds from higher financing costs and continued constraints in Des Moines due to power availability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. wheat surges to highest since 2024 as dry weather, fertilizer costs weigh on supply
data.description:
U.S. wheat futures rallied to the highest in nearly two years Tuesday, as drought in U.S. Plains wheat fields pressures yields while soaring fertilizer costs have caused farmers to trim planting of nutrient-intensive crops such as grains.

CBOT wheat futures surged after the latest Crop Progress report from the U.S. Department of Agriculture said only 30% of the winter wheat crop is in good or excellent condition as of April 26.

"The data highlights strong early planting but ongoing concerns around wheat crop health," Joe Davis of Futures International said in a note, adding that spring wheat is also struggling to get put into the ground, per USDA data.

U.S. growers were poised to plant the fewest acres of wheat since record keeping began in 1919, as high costs for fertilizer, seeds, and equipment have made it difficult to turn a profit.

CBOT wheat (W_1:COM) for July delivery jumped 4.5% to $6.58 1/4 per bushel, touching the highest level since June 2024, while corn (C_1:COM) for July delivery rose 1.3% to $4.75 1/2 per bushel, and soybeans (S_1:COM) for July delivery declined 0.2% to $11.90 1/4 per bushel.

ETFs: (WEAT), (CORN), (SOYB), (DBA), (MOO)
